MSFT.title: AOC Targets Apple, Calls For Breakup Of 'Far Too Big' Companies After Tim Cook Defends Price Hikes: Need 'Consumer Protections' For People

macro_events.event_summary: Crude oil rallies back above $70/bbl as renewed U.S.-Iran military clashes reignite fears of Middle East supply disruption, particularly around the Strait of Hormuz.
primary_drivers: Geopolitical risk premium repricing into crude futures
Potential Strait of Hormuz transit disruption threatening ~20% of seaborne oil flows
Higher realized prices flow directly to upstream E&P revenue and tanker day-rates

beneficiaries.rationale: Largest US integrated with significant upstream leverage; every $10/bbl move in Brent adds roughly $4-5B to annual upstream earnings. Direct beneficiary of higher realized prices.
Permian pure-play E&P with high oil cut (~70%) and unhedged exposure to spot WTI; among the highest beta names to crude price moves in the S&P 500.
High-leverage Permian E&P; oil-weighted production and balance sheet leverage amplify equity sensitivity to crude price spikes. Buffett-owned, attracts momentum flows.
TechnipFMC — subsea equipment and services provider; Middle East supply scare drives near-term spot pricing and longer-term reshoring of non-OPEC offshore capex.

Frontline — crude tanker operator; Hormuz disruption forces longer-haul routings (Cape, Pacific rerouting) which tightens VLCC supply and spikes day-rates. Tanker equities historically rally hard on Iran flashpoints.
International Seaways — diversified crude/product tanker fleet; same Hormuz reroute thesis lifts ton-mile demand and TCE rates.
Cheniere — US LNG exporter; Middle East gas/LNG supply disruption fears (Qatar transits Hormuz) tighten global LNG and boost US export economics.
Halliburton — large Middle East services revenue mix but more importantly, sustained >$70 oil supports US shale completion activity and pricing power.

losers.rationale: Delta — jet fuel is ~25-30% of operating cost; airlines historically have weak hedge books and limited ability to pass through fuel surcharges in competitive domestic market. Most international exposure of US legacies.
United — similar jet fuel exposure to DAL with heavy long-haul international routing that gets compounded by potential Middle East airspace closures and rerouting costs.
Southwest — unhedged jet fuel exposure (abandoned hedging program); domestic-only network can't reroute around the issue, pure margin compression.
Carnival — bunker fuel is a major opex line; cruise operators have thin margins and limited fuel hedges. Middle East tensions also threaten Red Sea/Suez itineraries.
Dow Inc — ethylene/petrochemical producer whose feedstock (naphtha, LPG) is directly tied to crude; margin compression risk if product prices don't follow input costs up immediately.
Walmart — transportation/diesel cost passthrough is slow; sustained oil spike pressures consumer discretionary spend at low end of income distribution where WMT overindexes.
FedEx — jet fuel and diesel are dominant variable costs across air and ground networks; fuel surcharge resets lag spot price moves by weeks.

headlines: Oil jumps back above $70 as fresh U.S.-Iran clashes reignite Middle East supply fears
